QAM Virtual Laboratory

A virtual laboratory experiment for studying Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM), focusing on constellation behavior, time-domain waveforms, and BER/SER performance over AWGN channels.

Aim

To study and simulate Quadrature Amplitude Modulation (QAM) and understand how varying SNR, carrier frequency, and symbol rate affects the BER and constellation diagram in AWGN channels.

Objectives

  • Understand QAM as a combined amplitude and phase modulation technique
  • Visualize how modulation order (M) affects spectral efficiency and noise tolerance
  • Analyze constellation diagrams, eye patterns, and I/Q baseband waveforms
  • Compare the performance of QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, and 256-QAM under AWGN
  • Observe the relationship between SNR and Bit Error Rate (BER)

Apparatus / Tools Required

  • QAM Virtual Lab — Web-based simulation environment (this tool)
  • Simulation Engine — Constellation diagram & eye pattern generator
  • CRO Waveform Viewer — Virtual oscilloscope for CH1 and CH2 signals
  • I/Q Signal Analyzer — Baseband component visualizer
  • Signal Computation Panel — Real-time QAM parameter calculator

Expected Outcomes

  • Understand the mathematical model of QAM signal generation
  • Observe and interpret a QAM constellation diagram at various SNR levels
  • Recognize the visual difference between clean (high SNR) and noisy (low SNR) constellations
  • Understand how the eye diagram reflects signal quality
  • Appreciate trade-offs between data rate and error performance in M-QAM

Introduction to QAM

QAM is a modulation technique that simultaneously varies both the amplitude and phase of a carrier signal to encode digital information. It achieves this by combining two orthogonal carriers (cosine and sine) — called the In-phase (I) and Quadrature (Q) components — each modulated independently by different amplitude levels.

Unlike ASK or PSK alone, QAM exploits both dimensions of the signal space, allowing significantly more bits to be transmitted per symbol, making it the modulation of choice in modern broadband systems like 4G, 5G, Wi-Fi, and cable television.

Key Equations

Only three equations matter for understanding QAM. Each is explained in plain language alongside the formula.

1 — The QAM Signal

A QAM signal combines two sine waves that are 90° apart — one called I (In-phase) and the other Q (Quadrature). Each (I, Q) pair forms a unique dot on the constellation diagram, encoding a specific group of bits.

QAM Transmitted Signal
s(t) = I(t) · cos(2πfct) − Q(t) · sin(2πfct)
I(t) = in-phase amplitude  |  Q(t) = quadrature amplitude  |  fc = carrier frequency

Changing I and Q values changes both the amplitude and phase of the carrier — this is how different bit patterns are encoded.

2 — Bits per Symbol

The more symbols a QAM scheme uses, the more bits each symbol carries. Doubling the number of symbols adds only one extra bit per symbol.

Bits per Symbol
k = log₂(M)    e.g. 16-QAM→k=4  |  64-QAM→k=6  |  256-QAM→k=8

More bits per symbol = higher throughput — but symbols are packed closer together on the constellation, making them harder to distinguish when noisy.

3 — Bit Rate vs Symbol Rate

Total bit rate depends on how fast symbols are sent and how many bits each carries. Higher-order QAM increases data rate without needing a faster symbol clock.

Bit Rate
Rb = Rs × k    where Rs = symbol rate (baud), k = bits per symbol

You will see this in the simulation: switching from 16-QAM to 64-QAM increases the Data Rate in the stats panel with the same symbol rate setting.

Constellation Diagram Examples

Left: QPSK (4 symbols, 2 bits/symbol) — Right: 16-QAM (16 symbols, 4 bits/symbol). Higher order QAM packs more points closer together, requiring better SNR.

Applications of QAM

QAM is the modulation scheme behind almost every fast digital link you use today.

4G LTE & 5G

Uses 16-QAM to 256-QAM adaptively. When signal is strong, the network switches to higher-order QAM for faster speeds.

Wi-Fi 5 & Wi-Fi 6

802.11ac uses 256-QAM. Wi-Fi 6 (802.11ax) pushes to 1024-QAM — 10 bits per symbol over clean indoor channels.

Cable TV & DOCSIS

DOCSIS 3.1 uses 64-QAM to 4096-QAM across hundreds of sub-carriers to deliver gigabit speeds over coaxial cable.

Satellite Links

Long-range links use QPSK or 8-QAM. Signals travel thousands of km and arrive weakly, requiring simpler constellations.

DSL Broadband

ADSL and VDSL use QAM with DMT — hundreds of QAM sub-carriers over a single copper telephone line to maximise data rate.

Digital TV & DAB Radio

DVB-T and DAB use QAM variants (COFDM) to broadcast HD video and digital audio over the air to millions of receivers.

Key Concepts

  • Spectral Efficiency: Higher M → more bits/Hz but demands higher SNR
  • Eye Diagram: Overlapping waveform traces — wider "eye opening" = better signal quality and lower ISI
  • AWGN Channel: Additive White Gaussian Noise — standard linear channel model for communications
  • Gray Coding: Adjacent constellation points differ by only 1 bit — minimizes BER for symbol errors
  • Symbol Rate (Baud): Symbol transitions per second; Bit Rate = Baud × log₂(M)
  • Coherent Detection: Receiver uses a local oscillator synchronized with the transmitter carrier
  • Minimum Distance: Closer constellation points → higher error probability in noise

Procedure

Follow the steps below carefully to complete the QAM Virtual Lab experiment. The procedure is divided into phases corresponding to the tabs in this application.

Phase 1 — Preparation

1

Read the Aim

Navigate to the Aim tab. Read the experiment objectives and understand what you are expected to observe and learn from this simulation.

2

Study the Theory

Navigate to the Theory tab. Study the QAM signal equation, transmitter and receiver block diagrams, and key concepts. Pay special attention to the equations for BER and symbol energy. Understand what I and Q components represent physically.

3

Complete the Pre-Test

Navigate to the Pre-Test tab. Answer all 5 questions honestly without referring to notes — this measures your prior knowledge. Submit and review the explanations for any incorrect answers.

Phase 2 — Constellation & Eye Diagram Simulation

4

Open the Simulation Tab

Navigate to the Simulation tab. You will see the QAM Constellation section at the top and the CRO Waveform Viewer below it.

5

Experiment with Modulation Order

Use the Modulation dropdown to select QPSK, then 16-QAM, 64-QAM, and 256-QAM. Click RUN SIMULATION after each change. Observe:

  • How the number of points in the constellation changes
  • How closely spaced the points become at higher M
  • The BER estimate displayed in the stats row
6

Vary the SNR Slider

Set the modulation to 16-QAM. Move the SNR slider from 0 dB to 30 dB in increments. At each setting, observe:

  • The spread of constellation points (tighter cluster = better SNR)
  • The eye diagram — does it open or close with changing SNR?
  • The BER value in the stats row — how does it change?

Note: At SNR ≈ 5 dB the constellation is very noisy. At SNR ≈ 25 dB it is nearly perfect.

7

Vary Amplitude

Keep SNR fixed at 15 dB and vary the Amplitude slider. Observe how the constellation scales in size while the relative noise remains proportional.

8

Click the HELP Button

Click the HELP bulb button in the simulation control panel. Reference the flashcard values shown and compare them with your current slider settings. Use these as starting reference points.

Phase 3 — CRO Waveform Analysis

9

Configure CRO Parameters

Scroll to the CRO Waveform Viewer section. Set the following starting values using the sliders:

  • Carrier Frequency: 100 Hz
  • Symbol Rate: 10 sym/s
  • Num Symbols: 8
  • CH1 Frequency: 100 Hz (same as carrier)
  • CH1 Amplitude: 1.0
  • CH1 Phase: 0°

Click ▶ GENERATE WAVEFORMS.

10

Observe the Oscilloscope

In the oscilloscope (top canvas), CH1 (green) shows the reference sinusoidal input and CH2 (cyan) shows the QAM modulated output. Toggle CH1 and CH2 on/off using the checkboxes to examine each signal independently.

11

Use the Zoom Feature

Use the + and − zoom buttons on the oscilloscope header to zoom into the waveform. Zooming in reveals individual symbol transitions in the QAM signal. Click RST to reset zoom.

12

Examine I/Q Baseband Components

Observe the I/Q Baseband Components canvas. The green step-wise waveform is I(t) and the cyan one is Q(t). Each step represents one symbol. Use the I/Q zoom buttons to examine individual symbols more closely.

13

Change Modulation Type in CRO

Switch the CRO modulation to 256-QAM and re-generate. Observe more amplitude levels in the I/Q panel and a more complex QAM waveform in CH2. Review the Signal Computation panel on the right for updated parameters.

14

Vary CH1 Phase

Move the Phase (°) slider for CH1 to ±90° or ±180°. Observe how the reference waveform shifts relative to the QAM output. This demonstrates carrier phase alignment.

Phase 4 — Post-Simulation Testing & Results

15

Complete the Post-Test

Navigate to the Post-Test tab. Answer all 5 questions based on what you observed during the simulation. Submit and review any incorrect answers with explanations.

16

View the Result Page

Navigate to the Result tab to see your Pre-Test vs Post-Test score comparison, knowledge improvement percentage, key learning outcomes, and the complete modulation comparison table.

️ Precautions

  • Complete the Pre-Test before opening the Simulation tab for honest baseline assessment
  • Change only one parameter at a time and observe the effect before changing another
  • Note down BER values at each modulation order for comparison in the results
  • Ensure carrier frequency is always higher than the symbol rate for valid QAM generation
  • Do not refresh the page between Pre-Test and Post-Test — scores are stored in session memory

Pre-Test — Before the Simulation

Answer all 5 questions and click Submit. You will see whether each answer is correct or incorrect, with an explanation. This helps gauge your prior knowledge before exploring the simulation.

Try SNR=5 dB to see constellation spread. Then increase to 25 dB to see clean clusters.

Complete both Pre-Test and Post-Test to see your progress.

Key Learning Outcomes

1. QAM Signal Construction

A QAM signal s(t) = I(t)·cos(2πfct) − Q(t)·sin(2πfct) encodes information in both amplitude and phase by independently modulating two orthogonal carriers.

2. Modulation Order vs. Spectral Efficiency

Higher-order QAM (e.g. 256-QAM) encodes more bits/symbol (log₂M), increasing data rate but requiring much higher SNR to maintain acceptable BER.

3. Constellation Diagrams

Points on a constellation diagram represent unique symbols. Higher SNR → tightly clustered received points. Low SNR → spread-out noisy cloud, causing symbol errors.

4. Eye Diagram Interpretation

An open "eye" in the eye diagram indicates good signal quality. Closed or narrow eyes indicate inter-symbol interference (ISI) or high noise.

5. I/Q Components

The I (in-phase) and Q (quadrature) baseband components together fully describe a QAM signal. They are recovered at the receiver using coherent detection with matched local oscillators.

Modulation Comparison Table

Modulation Symbols (M) Bits/Symbol (k) Spectral Efficiency Min SNR (Typical) Application
QPSK42Low~7 dBSatellite, deep space
16-QAM164Medium~14 dB4G LTE, Wi-Fi
64-QAM646High~21 dBCable TV, 5G
256-QAM2568Very High~28 dBFiber, DOCSIS 3.1

What We Observed in This Experiment

Through the simulation, we observed that:

  • Reducing SNR causes the constellation points to spread outward, increasing symbol error probability
  • Increasing modulation order (QPSK → 256-QAM) places symbols closer together, making them harder to distinguish in noise
  • The eye diagram "closes" as SNR decreases — a visual indicator of signal degradation
  • The I and Q baseband components are independent step-wise signals that jointly define each QAM symbol
  • The CRO oscilloscope shows the composite QAM RF signal vs. the reference sinusoidal input
